mnemonics.title: C.O.P. - Class Holds Objects and Properties
mnemonics.content: Class = Blueprint, Object = Instance created from blueprint. 'C' stands for Class (the template), 'O' stands for Object (the instance), 'P' stands for Properties (data members and member functions)

mnemonics.title: P.A.P. - Public Allows Pass, Private Prevents, Protected Partial
mnemonics.content: Access Specifiers: Public - accessible everywhere; Private - accessible only within class; Protected - accessible within class and derived classes

mnemonics.title: S.A.F.E. - Static Auto From Extern
mnemonics.content: Storage Class Modifiers: S=Static (lifetime throughout program), A=Auto (local, automatic), F=Fixed/Extern (external linkage), E=External/Extended

mnemonics.title: C.V.C. - Constant Volatile Cannot be Changed
mnemonics.content: Type Qualifiers: C=Const (value cannot change), V=Volatile (can change unexpectedly), C=Const Volatile (both properties)

mnemonics.title: D.A.R. - Define Allocate Repeat
mnemonics.content: Object Creation: D=Define class first, A=Allocate memory (or stack allocation), R=Repeat to create multiple objects from same class

acronyms.usage: Paradigm where classes and objects are fundamental - used when designing C++ programs using classes
System implementing OOP concepts including encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction
Special member functions - Constructor initializes objects, Destructor cleans up when object goes out of scope
Encapsulation principle - keep data private, provide public functions to access/modify data
Function/Class Modifiers: Inline (expand at call site), Private (default for classes), Explicit (prevents implicit conversions)

visualTips.visualization: Think of a class as an architectural blueprint for a house. The blueprint shows all rooms, doors, windows - but it's not a real house yet. The actual house built from that blueprint is the Object.
Visualize a cookie cutter (Class) and cookies (Objects). One cutter creates many identical-shaped cookies. Each cookie is a separate instance but shares the same structure.
Imagine a house (Class): Private = walls inside rooms (only occupants can access), Public = main door (everyone can enter), Protected = windows (only family/derived classes can use)
Think of modifiers as ID cards that define object properties: Const = 'Read Only' badge, Static = 'Permanent Resident' (exists throughout program), Volatile = 'Breaking News' (can change anytime)
Visualize a mirror showing 'this' object. 'this' is like pointing to yourself - it always refers to the current object calling the member function

analogies.analogy: Class is like a recipe card for a cake - it contains instructions and ingredients (properties/methods). Object is like the actual cake baked using that recipe. One recipe can make many cakes, just as one class can create many objects.
Public members are like the storefront of a shop - anyone from outside can see and use what's displayed there (accessible to all).
Private members are like the back office storage - only employees (class members) can access and manage the inventory directly.
Protected members are like a family heirloom - only immediate family (derived classes) can access it, not outsiders (other functions/classes).
Const is like a museum exhibit - marked 'Do Not Touch'. Once declared constant, its value cannot be changed, only viewed.
Static is like a whiteboard in a classroom - it exists even when no one is using it, and is shared among all students (class-level, not object-level).
Constructor is like the opening ceremony of a building - it sets up everything (initializes data) before the building (object) becomes functional.
Destructor is like the closing time cleanup - it locks up, turns off lights, and clears everything when the building (object) is no longer needed.
